saige_tier_reasoning: Autonomy confirmed: controller.py:26 chain.invoke(user_message) and orchestrator.py:87 chain.invoke({"input": peer_message}) are non-deterministic LLM-driven decision points. State-changing operations confirmed: tools.py:43 requests.post to billing-api.internal (customer subscription cancellation), notifications.py:41 sns.publish to support-replies SNS topic. Both reach customer-facing systems (cancel_subscription mutates billing state visible to the customer; SNS support-replies delivers email to customer_email). Tier 3 because autonomous LLM decision-making + state-changing operations that are customer-facing.

findings.message: Untrusted URL from HTTP request body fed directly into WebBaseLoader without hostname allowlist — attacker-controlled documents become attacker-controlled prompts (indirect prompt injection, OWASP LLM01).
findings.reasoning: controller.py:33 reads article_url = request.json.get('article_url') directly from the request body. Line 35 passes it to WebBaseLoader(article_url).load() with no hostname validation or allowlist check. The fetched document content then flows to chain.invoke at line 39 — attacker-controlled web content becomes the effective LLM input without any sanitisation boundary.
findings.remediation: Validate article_url against an allowlist of permitted hostnames before passing to WebBaseLoader. Reject any URL not on the allowlist with a 400. For corporate use, restrict to internal document stores (SharePoint, Confluence) with auth.

findings.message: Sole input guardrail is a keyword deny-list with no semantic/LLM-classifier backing — bypassable by indirect framing, role-play, base64 encoding, or simple rephrasing. This is the specific defect the partial-defence-bypass emulator campaign exploits.
findings.reasoning: input_filter.py:10-19 defines _DENY_PATTERNS as 9 static regexes. The is_safe() function at line 21 returns True for any string not matching these exact patterns — no semantic scoring, no embedding similarity, no model-backed classifier. Role-play framing ('pretend you are'), base64, or synonym substitution all bypass the list without triggering a match.
findings.remediation: Back the keyword filter with a semantic intent classifier — a second LLM scoring the input for injection intent, or a sentence-transformer similarity check against known attack intents. Rate-limit repeated rephrasings of the same blocked intent within the same session. The keyword list remains useful as a fast first pass; the semantic classifier is the backstop.

findings.message: Hardcoded database connection string with plaintext credentials in source — username 'demo' and password 'demo' will appear in git history, container images, and CI logs. CWE-798.
findings.reasoning: config.py:13 assigns DATABASE_URL as a string literal containing the full PostgreSQL DSN including username and password. There is no os.environ reference, no secrets manager call, and no indirection of any kind. Any CI job that prints environment variables, any container inspection, or any git history traversal exposes these credentials.
findings.remediation: Move DATABASE_URL to an environment variable (os.environ['DATABASE_URL']) or a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager / HashiCorp Vault). Rotate the credentials — they are already compromised if this file has been committed to any git repository.

findings.message: cancel_subscription is a destructive tool (billing mutation, customer-visible state change) with no HITL confirmation gate — the agent can invoke it autonomously. The numeric-ID validation is not an approval gate; it is an injection guard only.
findings.reasoning: tools.py:26-43 defines cancel_subscription as a @tool. Line 36 validates customer_id.isdigit() — this prevents trivial injection but is not a human approval gate. Line 43 makes the requests.post to billing-api.internal unconditionally once invoked. No HumanApprovalCallbackHandler, no interrupt_before, and no out-of-band confirmation step exists anywhere in the call path. Tier 1 rule DF004 fired correctly; the numeric check creates a false sense of safety without adding approval semantics.
findings.remediation: Wire a HumanApprovalCallbackHandler (LangChain) or an interrupt_before= node (LangGraph) before cancel_subscription executes. Any tool whose name contains delete_*, cancel_*, charge_*, or similar destructive verbs must require out-of-band human confirmation before the side-effecting call runs.

findings.message: calculate tool has no args_schema= Pydantic model — the LLM guesses the argument shape, making the tool surface wider than intended.
findings.reasoning: tools.py:12 defines calculate with a bare string parameter and no args_schema=. Without a Pydantic model, the LLM planner receives no field-level constraints and can pass arbitrary content — including injected sub-commands or multi-line expressions — as the expression argument.
findings.remediation: Add args_schema=CalculateInput where CalculateInput is a Pydantic BaseModel with expression: str = Field(description='A safe arithmetic expression'). This constrains what the planner can pass.

findings.message: cancel_subscription tool has no args_schema= Pydantic model — no type-level constraint stops the planner from passing non-numeric or compound values before the runtime isdigit() check.
findings.reasoning: tools.py:25 defines cancel_subscription with no args_schema=. The isdigit() check at line 36 fires at runtime after dispatch — it cannot prevent the planner from constructing a malformed customer_id that probes the billing API in unexpected ways. Schema-level validation would reject non-numeric inputs before the tool body executes.
findings.remediation: Add args_schema=CancelInput where CancelInput validates customer_id: str = Field(pattern=r'^\d+$'). Pydantic validation at schema level is a stronger guarantee than runtime isdigit().

findings.snippet:
def send_reply(customer_email: str, ticket_summary: str) -> None:
    response = client.chat.completions.create(...)
findings.message: LLM call in send_reply has no surrounding audit logging — no logger setup, no structured trace, no callbacks. There is no record of what prompt was sent, what reply was generated, or that the SNS publish occurred.
findings.reasoning: notifications.py has no import of logging, structlog, or any tracing library. send_reply at line 29 calls client.chat.completions.create() and sns.publish() at line 41 with no log statements wrapping either call. If a malicious notification is sent, the application has no internal record of the prompt, the generated content, or the recipient — incident response depends entirely on SNS delivery logs which the application does not control.
findings.remediation: Add structured logging before and after the LLM call: log the hashed prompt, model ID, token counts, and reply hash. Log the SNS publish with the message hash and recipient. Use OpenTelemetry or LangSmith for distributed tracing if this runs inside an agent loop.

findings.message: The /chat handler invokes the LLM at line 26 with no audit logging — no logger in the file, no callbacks kwarg, no trace instrumentation. Incident response requires knowing what each request sent and received.
findings.reasoning: controller.py imports no logging or tracing library. The chat() handler at line 18 calls chain.invoke(user_message) at line 26 with no callbacks= argument and no log statement before or after. Every LLM interaction — user message, model, response — produces no durable application-side record. The /summarise handler at line 30 has the same gap.
findings.remediation: Add a structured logger (e.g. structlog or stdlib logging) and log the hashed user_message and hashed response.content on every call. At minimum: request ID, timestamp, input hash, output hash, model, latency.

findings.message: LLM call in send_reply has no explicit timeout — relies on OpenAI SDK default, which can hang indefinitely on slow completions and block the notification pipeline.
findings.reasoning: notifications.py:30 calls client.chat.completions.create(model=MODEL, messages=[...]) with no timeout= or max_tokens= parameter. The OpenAI SDK's default timeout is effectively unbounded on older SDK versions. A slow or stuck completion will block the send_reply call — and by extension the SNS publish — for an indeterminate time, stalling the notification pipeline.
findings.remediation: Pass timeout= to the create() call (e.g. timeout=30) and set max_tokens= to cap reply length. For the OpenAI SDK: client.chat.completions.create(..., timeout=30, max_tokens=1000).

findings.message: ChatOpenAI instantiated at module level with no timeout or max_tokens — every LLM call in the /chat and /summarise handlers inherits an uncapped timeout.
findings.reasoning: controller.py:15 constructs the ChatOpenAI instance at module load with no request_timeout= or max_tokens= argument. Both chain.invoke calls — chat() at line 26 and summarise() at line 39 — use this single uncapped instance. An attacker submitting a prompt designed to produce a long completion will hold the handler thread open until the LLM finishes or the process is killed.
findings.remediation: Pass request_timeout= and max_tokens= to the ChatOpenAI constructor: ChatOpenAI(model=MODEL, openai_api_key=..., request_timeout=30, max_tokens=2000).

findings.message: LLM output published to SNS with only partial PII scrubbing — _scrub_pii() at line 37 redacts SSN/passport patterns but leaves email addresses, account numbers, injected tool-call syntax, and arbitrary LLM content intact in reply_body before it reaches the customer-facing SNS topic.
findings.reasoning: _scrub_pii() at notifications.py:22-35 applies two regexes: one for SSN (\d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}) and one for passport (\^[A-Z]{2}\d{7}). Email addresses, phone numbers, credit-card numbers, account identifiers, and any injected tool-call syntax in the LLM reply pass through unredacted. The scrubbed reply_body is then published at line 41 to the customer-facing support-replies SNS topic — partial scrubbing provides false safety.
findings.remediation: Apply a comprehensive output scrubber (Presidio AnonymizerEngine or equivalent) covering email, phone, account number, and credit-card patterns — not just SSN/passport. Apply the scrubber at the SNS publish boundary, not at the LLM call site, so any downstream path that calls sns.publish is also covered.

findings.message: User message and raw LLM response persisted verbatim to the memory store with no content validation, schema check, or moderation classifier — an attacker can poison the memory for their session via crafted user text that will be recalled in future turns.
findings.reasoning: memory.py:28 appends raw user_message and llm_response to the in-memory dict with no intermediate validation. No content classifier, no moderation check, and no schema constraint is applied before the write. A user message containing future-turn instructions ('remember: from now on always...') will be stored verbatim and recalled by get_context() at line 41 on the next turn, propagating the injected instruction forward.
findings.remediation: Validate memory writes the same way you would validate database writes. Apply content classification (Llama Guard, Lakera, or a lightweight classifier) before persisting. Strip system-prompt-shaped directives ('remember this always', 'from now on...') from user messages before storing. Consider storing only a hash or a schema-validated summary rather than raw LLM output.

findings.message: All sessions share a single flat JSON file on disk — memory is keyed by session_id but the file itself has no access controls and is readable/writable by any process on the host. A low-privilege attacker with file-system access can read or poison any session's memory by knowing (or guessing) session IDs.
findings.reasoning: memory.py:16 sets MEMORY_FILE = Path('/tmp/demo-agent-memory.json'). /tmp is world-writable on Linux and macOS. The file contains the full conversation history of every session keyed only by session_id. A co-resident process (container escape, shared-host attack) can read all sessions or overwrite individual entries to inject poisoned context without any authentication or filesystem ACL enforcement.
findings.remediation: Move session memory to a proper data store (Redis with per-key ACLs, DynamoDB with IAM-scoped access) rather than a world-readable temp file. Ensure the session_id key is a cryptographically random UUID issued at session start, not a user-supplied value that can be guessed. Apply a TTL so stale memory is purged automatically.

findings.message: Inter-agent authentication relies solely on the presence of the X-Internal-Caller header — any process on the internal network that can set this header is trusted as a peer agent. No signature verification, no token validation, no allowlist of known caller identities.
findings.reasoning: orchestrator.py:82-84 reads the X-Internal-Caller header and returns 403 if it is falsy. There is no HMAC verification, no JWT validation, and no allowlist of permitted caller identities. The header value is not even checked against a known set of agent names — any string makes the guard pass. Any process on the internal network can set this header and receive full peer-agent authority.
findings.remediation: Replace header-presence checks with signed JWT verification: each peer agent presents a short-lived JWT signed with its private key; receive_from_peer verifies the signature and checks the issuer claim against an allowlist of known agent identities. Reject unsigned or unrecognised callers with 401. Log every inter-agent call with the verified caller identity.

findings.message: Tool authority in receive_from_peer is gated only on an unauthenticated header — any caller claiming to be an internal agent gets full LLM access. A spoofed X-Internal-Caller header bypasses the 403 gate entirely and grants the attacker the same authority as a trusted peer.
findings.reasoning: The 403 gate at orchestrator.py:82-84 is the sole access control for receive_from_peer. Passing the gate — which requires only a non-empty string in X-Internal-Caller — grants the caller chain.invoke access at line 87 with no further capability scoping. Because the header is set by the caller, it is trivially forgeable. The attacker gains full LLM-agent authority with no cryptographic proof of identity.
findings.remediation: Bind tool and LLM access authority to a cryptographically verified caller identity (mTLS, signed JWT), not a string header. The decision to trust a caller must be made by a deterministic auth layer, never by the presence of a header the caller sets themselves. See also TIER2-AGENTIC-T9-03.

findings.snippet:
user_input = request.json.get("message", "")
forward_response = requests.post(
    DOWNSTREAM_AGENT_URL,
    json={"message": user_input},
    headers={"X-Internal-Caller": "orchestrator"},
    timeout=30,
)
findings.message: delegate() chains user input directly to a downstream agent call with no per-tool egress policy — the tool chain (user → orchestrator → downstream agent) has no policy check between dispatches. An attacker who can influence user_input can direct arbitrary payloads to the downstream agent under the orchestrator's authority.
findings.reasoning: orchestrator.py:62 reads user_input = request.json.get('message', '') with no sanitisation. Lines 63-67 forward it verbatim to DOWNSTREAM_AGENT_URL via requests.post with the X-Internal-Caller: orchestrator header, granting the forwarded payload orchestrator-level authority at the receiving agent. No egress policy checks the destination URL; no input scrubber or intent classifier sits between the user's message and the downstream agent call.
findings.remediation: Apply an input sanitiser to user_input before forwarding to the downstream agent. Enforce egress policy: validate the destination URL against an allowlist before each dispatch. Log every cross-agent chain so attacker recon followed by exfil attempts trigger an alert.

findings.message: Memory isolation relies entirely on the caller passing the correct session_id — there is no data-layer enforcement. Any caller who knows (or guesses) another session's ID can read that session's full conversation history by passing it to recall(). The tenant boundary is in the application, not at the storage layer.
findings.reasoning: memory.py:41 reads the entire MEMORY_FILE and returns the entry keyed by session_id. The session_id is the sole access key and is passed by the caller — it is not bound to an authenticated user identity or a cryptographic token. An attacker who enumerates or guesses a valid session_id (e.g., via timing, sequential IDs, or leaked values) can read that session's full conversation history with no further auth check.
findings.remediation: Enforce the session boundary at the data-access layer: use a database (Redis, DynamoDB) where each session's memory is stored under a key derived from both the session_id and the authenticated user identity, and access requires IAM/auth credentials scoped to that key. session_id alone is not a secret.

findings.message: The audit trail for the send_reply action is absent — there is no immutable log entry recording that sns.publish was called, what the message contained (hash), or which customer_email was targeted. If the agent later denies sending a notification, there is no out-of-band record to confirm or refute it.
findings.reasoning: notifications.py:37-41 applies _scrub_pii() and calls sns.publish() with no surrounding log statements. The application produces no record of the SNS publish event, the scrubbed message content, or the recipient. Non-repudiation requires an immutable out-of-band record the agent cannot modify — relying on SNS delivery logs alone is insufficient because the application has no control over their retention or integrity.
findings.remediation: Emit an immutable structured audit log entry before sns.publish returns: timestamp, hashed message body, recipient hash, SNS topic ARN, and the calling principal. Write to an append-only store (CloudWatch Logs with no-delete policy, S3 with Object Lock) that the agent process cannot modify. Never derive the audit trail from the agent's self-report.

findings.message: The orchestrator has no circuit breaker on consecutive LLM call failures and no externally-triggerable kill switch — if chain.invoke begins producing errors or loops (e.g. due to a runaway attacker session), there is no mechanism to drain or stop the agent without killing the process.
findings.reasoning: receive_from_peer at orchestrator.py:72-92 calls chain.invoke at line 87 on every inbound request with no retry counter, no failure threshold, no circuit breaker, and no externally-accessible drain toggle. A single attacker session flooding the endpoint with requests that cause LLM errors or loops will exhaust quota and tie up threads with no automatic remediation — the only recourse is a hard process kill.
findings.remediation: Wrap chain.invoke in a circuit breaker (pybreaker) that opens after N consecutive failures. Expose a kill switch (feature flag, SIGTERM handler, or queue-drain toggle) so an operator can stop accepting new requests without a hard kill. Alert when the circuit opens.

findings.message: delegate() passes user_input to a downstream agent and returns its response verbatim to the caller with no schema check, no verification step, and no 'did the downstream agent do what was intended' eval — cascading hallucination / manipulation is possible at the hand-off.
findings.reasoning: orchestrator.py:63-67 POSTs user_input to DOWNSTREAM_AGENT_URL and at line 68-69 returns forward_response.json() verbatim to the original caller. There is no schema validation on the downstream response, no intent-consistency check, and no error-status inspection. A manipulated or hallucinating downstream agent can inject arbitrary content into the orchestrator's response without triggering any alarm.
findings.remediation: Validate the downstream agent's response against a typed schema before returning it. For high-stakes outputs, add a classifier or second LLM-as-judge step that checks whether the downstream response is consistent with the original intent.

findings.snippet:
reply = chain.invoke({"input": peer_message})
return jsonify({"reply": reply.get("text", ""), "caller": caller})
findings.message: receive_from_peer logs neither the caller identity, the peer_message content, nor the LLM reply — there is no audit trail of what instructions the peer sent or what the agent decided. A compromised peer agent leaves no trace.
findings.reasoning: orchestrator.py:72-92 defines receive_from_peer. There are no import statements for logging or tracing libraries in orchestrator.py. chain.invoke at line 87 and the return at line 88 have no surrounding log calls. The caller value from the (unauthenticated) header is echoed back to the caller but never written to any audit log — a compromised peer agent operating through this handler leaves no application-side trace.
findings.remediation: Log every inter-agent call: verified caller identity, hashed peer_message, model, latency, hashed reply. Use a structured logger and treat inter-agent calls as security-relevant events requiring the same audit rigour as user-facing actions.

tier1_fp_callouts.reasoning: config.py:7 passes api_key="sk-proj-DEMO1234567890abcdef" as a string literal to the OpenAI() constructor. No env-var indirection, no secrets manager reference. Confirmed real credential exposure.

is_safe() is called at controller.py:23 before chain.invoke at line 26 — a guardrail is present. However, guard/input_filter.py:10-19 shows it is a keyword deny-list only, with no semantic classifier. The guardrail is real but shallow; the input still reaches the LLM under indirect or obfuscated framings. Context-dependent: partially mitigated, not fully.

controller.py:26 processes requests from any caller — the /chat route has no peer-origin check. The keyword filter at is_safe() does not distinguish between user and peer input. Confirmed: no peer identity validation at this entry point.

controller.py:35 loads article_url directly from request.json with no hostname allowlist, then controller.py:39 feeds the loaded document content into chain.invoke. No sanitiser between URL fetch and LLM call. Confirmed real.

The /summarise endpoint does not explicitly handle peer-sourced requests, but the URL parameter is user-supplied and unvalidated. The risk is indirect prompt injection via attacker-controlled document content rather than direct peer impersonation. Context-dependent — partially overlaps with LLM01-02 (indirect injection) rather than T13 (rogue agent) specifically.

orchestrator.py:62-65: user_input from request.json flows directly into the requests.post body to DOWNSTREAM_AGENT_URL with no sanitisation. The X-Internal-Caller header grants the forwarded payload orchestrator-level authority. Confirmed T12 — Agent Communication Poisoning.
orchestrator.py:85-87: peer_message from request.json flows to chain.invoke with no guardrail. No is_safe() equivalent is called in this handler. Confirmed real.

orchestrator.py:82-84: caller identity checked only by header presence (X-Internal-Caller). No signature, no token, no allowlist. Any process on the internal network is implicitly trusted. Confirmed T13 — Rogue Agents.
orchestrator.py:107-110: user_input from request.json passed to chain.invoke in debug_endpoint with no sanitiser. Confirmed real.
debug_endpoint at orchestrator.py:98 has no caller authentication — any HTTP client can POST to /api/orchestrator/debug without any identity check. Confirmed real.

orchestrator.py:116-118: the except block at line 112 constructs a response dict containing 'active_system_prompt': SYSTEM_PROMPT. SYSTEM_PROMPT (line 91-95) contains the embedded escalation key SK-OPS-7741-PRIVATE. This is returned in a 500 response body visible to the caller. Confirmed real — system prompt with embedded secret leaks on error.

tools.py:19-21: code = response.choices[0].message.content (raw LLM output) fed directly to eval(code). No AST parsing, no allowlist, no sandbox. Confirmed real — this is direct RCE via prompt injection.
SKILL.md frontmatter declares permissions.network: true with no network.allow allowlist. Confirmed unrestricted outbound network access.
SKILL.md declares a wildcard read path (~/.config/demo-agent/**). Confirmed — no explicit path enumeration.
SKILL.md frontmatter has no author.identity field. Confirmed absent.
SKILL.md frontmatter has no content_hash field. Confirmed absent.
SKILL.md frontmatter has no signature field. Confirmed absent.

SKILL.md grants network_egress; skills/billing/SKILL.md grants shell. Together these allow the agent to exec arbitrary commands and exfiltrate results over the network. Confirmed dangerous combination.
SKILL.md grants files_write; skills/billing/SKILL.md grants shell. Together these allow dropping arbitrary scripts to disk and executing them. Confirmed dangerous combination.
config.yaml:17 contains a hardcoded Anthropic API key. Confirmed real — this is a skill-bundle file that ships with the agent.
config.yaml:21 contains a hardcoded OpenAI project key. Confirmed real.
config.yaml:25 contains a hardcoded AWS access key ID. Confirmed real.
config.yaml:29 contains a generic hardcoded credential assignment. Confirmed real.
config.yaml:30 contains a hardcoded Slack token. Confirmed real.
skills/billing/SKILL.md declares shell: true. No justification or description of why the billing skill requires shell access. Confirmed.
skills/billing/SKILL.md has no author.identity field in frontmatter. Confirmed absent.
skills/billing/SKILL.md frontmatter has no content_hash field. Confirmed absent.
skills/billing/SKILL.md frontmatter has no signature field. Confirmed absent.
